MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report

IBM 6530 — Group 4

Authors

Min Gong

Jeffrey Hsu

Emily Kuo

Eunice Won

Ceren Unal

Published

May 2026

Links: GitHub Repo

1 Executive Summary

This report presents a descriptive and attribution-based analysis of website activity for the MSDM Odoo website, using Google Analytics 4 (GA4) event data exported from BigQuery. The analysis covers eight snapshot dates between February 9 and April 3, 2026.

Key findings:

  • The site recorded substantial engagement over the snapshot period, with desktop users making up the majority of traffic.
  • Direct and organic search are the dominant traffic channels by page views; however, attribution modeling reveals a more nuanced picture.
  • A Markov chain attribution model substantially re-weights credit away from last-touch channels (Direct, Email) toward upper-funnel channels (Paid Social), suggesting that last-touch attribution systematically undervalues awareness-stage marketing.
  • The most actionable recommendation is to increase investment in Paid Social, which the Markov model credits at 15.8% vs. only 5.0% under last-touch.

Important caveat: This analysis is based on a frozen snapshot of 8 dates. Results are directional. Conversion events are not available in this GA4 extract, so attribution is modeled from page-view sequences as a proxy.

2 Business Problem

2.1 Client Context

The client is the MSDM (Master of Science in Data Management) program, which operates an informational and lead-generation website built on Odoo. The website serves prospective students, current applicants, and program stakeholders.

The program’s marketing team needs to understand:

  1. Where is traffic coming from? — Which channels and geographies are driving visits?
  2. Who is visiting? — Device usage, behavior, and engagement depth.
  3. Which channels actually drive conversions? — Beyond surface-level traffic, which channels contribute most along the full user journey?
  4. Where should marketing budget be focused? — Given limited resources, which channels have the highest return?

2.2 Why Standard Reporting Is Not Enough

The default view in Google Analytics uses last-touch attribution: the final channel a user visited before converting receives 100% of the credit. This model is simple but systematically misleading for programs like MSDM where:

  • Users typically discover the program through social media or organic search, then return via direct or email links to convert.
  • Upper-funnel channels (Paid Social, Organic) never receive credit under last-touch even if they were the original discovery source.
  • Budget decisions made on last-touch data tend to over-invest in closing channels and under-invest in awareness channels.

This report addresses that gap by combining descriptive reporting (from the GA4 snapshot) with a Markov chain attribution model that distributes credit across all touchpoints in the user journey.

3 Data Overview

3.1 Source and Pipeline

Show code 
library(arrow)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(lubridate)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(forcats)
library(fs)
library(here)
library(readr)
library(gt)
library(knitr)
library(ggrepel)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.width = 9, fig.height = 5)

# ── Paths ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA_DIR     <- here::here("data")
PARQUET_DIR  <- fs::path(DATA_DIR, "msdm_snapshot", "events_flat")
PARQUET_FILE <- fs::path(PARQUET_DIR, "events_snapshot.parquet")
CSV_PATH     <- fs::path(DATA_DIR, "events_snapshot.csv")

# ── Build parquet if needed ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if (!fs::file_exists(PARQUET_FILE)) {
  fs::dir_create(PARQUET_DIR)
  arrow::write_parquet(
    readr::read_csv(CSV_PATH, show_col_types = FALSE),
    PARQUET_FILE
  )
}

# ── Load dataset ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ds <- arrow::open_dataset(PARQUET_DIR) |> dplyr::collect()

BigQuery source: odoo-487003 / analytics_523126945

Pipeline:

BigQuery (GA4 raw tables)
   ↓  SQL extract (flat event schema)
events_snapshot.csv
   ↓  Apache Arrow / write_parquet()
events_snapshot.parquet
   ↓  R (arrow, dplyr, ggplot2)
Analysis + Dashboard + Report

All analysis in this report runs from a local Parquet snapshot. No live BigQuery connection is required after the initial export. This ensures the pipeline is stable and reproducible.

3.2 Coverage and Key Fields

Show code 
tibble::tibble(
  Item    = c("Date range", "Snapshot days", "BigQuery project",
              "BigQuery dataset", "Primary key", "Key fields"),
  Details = c(
    "February 9 – April 3, 2026",
    "8 days across ~7 weeks",
    "odoo-487003",
    "analytics_523126945",
    "user_pseudo_id (anonymous)",
    "event_name, traffic_medium, device_category, geo_country, event_date"
  )
) |>
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Item", "Details"))
Item Details
Date range February 9 – April 3, 2026
Snapshot days 8 days across ~7 weeks
BigQuery project odoo-487003
BigQuery dataset analytics_523126945
Primary key user_pseudo_id (anonymous)
Key fields event_name, traffic_medium, device_category, geo_country, event_date

3.3 Key Definitions

Term Definition
Unique Users Counted by user_pseudo_id (anonymous; resets across devices/browsers)
Traffic Medium First-touch medium from traffic_medium; (none) recoded as direct
Page Views Events where event_name == "page_view"
Conversion Not available in this snapshot; page-view sequences used as proxy for attribution
Session Not reconstructed; no timestamp-based sessionization applied

4 Descriptive Analysis

4.1 Pre-Computed Summaries

Show code 
# Traffic medium
traffic_summary <- ds |>
  filter(event_name == "page_view") |>
  mutate(
    medium_clean = case_when(
      is.na(traffic_medium)      ~ "(not set)",
      traffic_medium == "(none)" ~ "direct",
      TRUE                       ~ traffic_medium
    )
  ) |>
  count(medium_clean, name = "page_views") |>
  arrange(desc(page_views))

# Device
device_summary <- ds |>
  filter(!is.na(device_category)) |>
  group_by(device_category) |>
  summarise(
    n_events       = n(),
    n_unique_users = n_distinct(user_pseudo_id)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(n_events)) |>
  mutate(pct_events = n_events / sum(n_events) * 100)

# Events by date
events_by_date <- ds |>
  count(event_date, name = "n_events") |>
  arrange(event_date)

# Top event types
event_types <- ds |>
  count(event_name, name = "count") |>
  arrange(desc(count)) |>
  slice_head(n = 8)

# Geo
geo_summary <- ds |>
  filter(!is.na(geo_country), event_name == "page_view") |>
  count(geo_country, name = "page_views") |>
  arrange(desc(page_views)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

# KPIs
total_events    <- nrow(ds)
total_users     <- n_distinct(ds$user_pseudo_id)
total_pv        <- ds |> filter(event_name == "page_view") |> nrow()
events_per_user <- total_events / total_users
pv_per_user     <- total_pv / total_users
top_medium      <- traffic_summary$medium_clean[1]
top_device      <- device_summary$device_category[1]

4.2 KPI Summary

Show code 
tibble::tibble(
  Metric  = c("Total Events", "Unique Users", "Page Views",
              "Events per User", "Page Views per User",
              "Top Channel", "Top Device"),
  Value   = c(
    scales::comma(total_events),
    scales::comma(total_users),
    scales::comma(total_pv),
    round(events_per_user, 1),
    round(pv_per_user, 1),
    str_to_title(top_medium),
    str_to_title(top_device)
  )
) |>
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Metric", "Value"),
               caption = "Top-line KPIs from GA4 snapshot")
Top-line KPIs from GA4 snapshot
Metric Value
Total Events 193
Unique Users 8
Page Views 59
Events per User 24.1
Page Views per User 7.4
Top Channel Direct
Top Device Desktop

The average of 24.1 events per user and 7.4 page views per user suggests that visitors are interacting with multiple pages per session, which is consistent with an exploratory, research-mode audience typical of graduate program websites.

4.3 Traffic by Channel

Show code 
traffic_summary |>
  mutate(medium_clean = fct_reorder(medium_clean, page_views)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = medium_clean, y = page_views, fill = medium_clean)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(page_views)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Page Views",
       title = "Page Views by Traffic Medium",
       caption = "Filtered to page_view events; (none) recoded as direct") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
Show code 
traffic_summary |>
  mutate(pct = round(page_views / sum(page_views) * 100, 1)) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(medium_clean = "Medium", page_views = "Page Views", pct = "% Share") |>
  fmt_number(columns = page_views, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(columns = pct, decimals = 1) |>
  data_color(columns = page_views, palette = "Greens") |>
  tab_options(table.width = pct(100))
Medium Page Views % Share
direct 43 72.9
referral 14 23.7
(not set) 2 3.4

Traffic medium summary with percentage share.

Interpretation: Direct traffic accounts for the largest share of page views. However, a high proportion of Direct traffic is a common indicator of incomplete UTM tagging across campaigns — users who click untagged email or social links will appear as “direct” in GA4. This is addressed directly in the attribution section below.

4.4 Device Usage

Show code 
p1 <- device_summary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = "", y = pct_events, fill = device_category)) +
  geom_col(width = 1, color = "white") +
  coord_polar(theta = "y") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(device_category, "\n", round(pct_events, 1), "%")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 4, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Event Share by Device", fill = "Device") +
  theme_void()

p2 <- device_summary |>
  mutate(device_category = fct_reorder(device_category, n_unique_users)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = device_category, y = n_unique_users, fill = device_category)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n_unique_users)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Unique Users", title = "Unique Users by Device") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2)

Desktop dominates both event share and unique users, consistent with graduate program research behavior (users comparing programs, reading requirements, completing applications). Mobile unique users are non-trivial, however, and represent an audience that merits continued UX investment.

5 Advanced Analysis: Multi-Touch Attribution

5.1 Motivation

Last-touch attribution — the default in most analytics platforms — assigns 100% of conversion credit to the final channel before a conversion event. This is convenient but misleading for multi-step user journeys. For the MSDM website, a typical journey might look like:

[Paid Social ad] → [Organic search] → [Email link] → [Direct visit / convert]

Under last-touch, Direct receives all the credit. Paid Social and Organic receive none, even though they initiated and nurtured the journey.

5.2 Markov Chain Attribution Model

A Markov chain attribution model treats the user journey as a sequence of channel states. It estimates each channel’s contribution by computing the removal effect: how much would conversion probability drop if that channel were removed entirely from all journeys? Channels that appear early and often in high-converting paths receive proportionally more credit.

5.2.1 Attribution Results

Show code 
library(tidyr)

# ── Load attribution dataset ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
attr_raw <- readr::read_csv(
  here::here("data", "attribution_dataset.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

channel_cols <- grep("^channel", names(attr_raw), value = TRUE)
channels_list <- c("Direct", "Email", "Organic", "Paid Search", "Paid Social", "Referral")

# ── Last-Touch Attribution ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
converters <- attr_raw |> filter(user_purchase == 1)

last_touch_attr <- converters |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    last_ch = {
      vals <- c_across(all_of(channel_cols))
      vals <- vals[!is.na(vals)]
      if (length(vals) > 0) tail(vals, 1) else NA_character_
    }
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!is.na(last_ch)) |>
  count(last_ch, name = "credit") |>
  mutate(pct = round(credit / sum(credit) * 100, 2)) |>
  rename(Channel = last_ch, Last_Touch = pct) |>
  select(Channel, Last_Touch)

# ── First-Touch Attribution ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
first_touch_attr <- converters |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    first_ch = {
      vals <- c_across(all_of(channel_cols))
      vals <- vals[!is.na(vals)]
      if (length(vals) > 0) vals[1] else NA_character_
    }
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!is.na(first_ch)) |>
  count(first_ch, name = "credit") |>
  mutate(pct = round(credit / sum(credit) * 100, 2)) |>
  rename(Channel = first_ch, First_Touch = pct) |>
  select(Channel, First_Touch)

# ── Linear Attribution ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
linear_credits <- converters |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    path = list({
      vals <- c_across(all_of(channel_cols))
      vals[!is.na(vals)]
    }),
    n_ch = length(path)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(n_ch > 0) |>
  unnest(path) |>
  group_by(users_id, path, n_ch) |>
  summarise(.groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(credit = 1 / n_ch) |>
  group_by(path) |>
  summarise(total_credit = sum(credit), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(pct = round(total_credit / sum(total_credit) * 100, 2)) |>
  rename(Channel = path, Linear = pct) |>
  select(Channel, Linear)

# ── Markov Chain Attribution (Absorbing Chain / Removal Effect) ────────────────
# Build transition counts
build_transitions <- function(df, channel_cols) {
  trans <- list()
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(df))) {
    path <- unlist(df[i, channel_cols])
    path <- path[!is.na(path)]
    if (length(path) == 0) next
    outcome <- if (df$user_purchase[i] == 1) "conversion" else "null"
    
    # start -> first
    from <- "start"; to <- path[1]
    trans[[from]][[to]] <- (trans[[from]][[to]] %||% 0) + 1
    
    # channel -> channel
    if (length(path) > 1) {
      for (j in seq_len(length(path) - 1)) {
        from <- path[j]; to <- path[j+1]
        trans[[from]][[to]] <- (trans[[from]][[to]] %||% 0) + 1
      }
    }
    # last -> outcome
    from <- tail(path, 1)
    trans[[from]][[outcome]] <- (trans[[from]][[outcome]] %||% 0) + 1
  }
  trans
}

`%||%` <- function(a, b) if (!is.null(a)) a else b

trans_counts <- build_transitions(attr_raw, channel_cols)

# Convert to probabilities
trans_prob <- lapply(trans_counts, function(x) {
  total <- sum(unlist(x))
  lapply(x, function(v) v / total)
})

# Build Q and R matrices for absorbing chain
all_transient <- c(channels_list, "start")
absorbing     <- c("conversion", "null")
n_t <- length(all_transient)
state_idx <- setNames(seq_along(all_transient), all_transient)

Q <- matrix(0, n_t, n_t, dimnames = list(all_transient, all_transient))
R <- matrix(0, n_t, 2,   dimnames = list(all_transient, absorbing))

for (s in all_transient) {
  probs <- trans_prob[[s]]
  if (is.null(probs)) next
  for (t in names(probs)) {
    p <- probs[[t]]
    if (t %in% all_transient) {
      Q[s, t] <- p
    } else if (t %in% absorbing) {
      R[s, t] <- p
    }
  }
}

# Fundamental matrix and absorption probabilities
N_mat <- solve(diag(n_t) - Q)
B_mat <- N_mat %*% R

baseline_conv <- B_mat["start", "conversion"]

# Removal effect for each channel
removal_effects <- sapply(channels_list, function(ch) {
  Q2 <- Q; R2 <- R
  # Redirect transitions TO ch → null
  for (s in all_transient) {
    if (Q2[s, ch] > 0) {
      R2[s, "null"] <- R2[s, "null"] + Q2[s, ch]
      Q2[s, ch] <- 0
    }
  }
  # ch itself: absorb to null
  Q2[ch, ] <- 0
  R2[ch, ] <- c(0, 1)
  
  tryCatch({
    N2 <- solve(diag(n_t) - Q2)
    B2 <- N2 %*% R2
    max(0, baseline_conv - B2["start", "conversion"])
  }, error = function(e) 0)
})

markov_pct_vals <- round(removal_effects / sum(removal_effects) * 100, 2)

markov_attr <- data.frame(
  Channel = names(markov_pct_vals),
  Markov  = as.numeric(markov_pct_vals)
)

# ── Combined comparison table ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
comparison <- last_touch_attr |>
  full_join(first_touch_attr, by = "Channel") |>
  full_join(linear_credits,   by = "Channel") |>
  full_join(markov_attr,      by = "Channel") |>
  arrange(desc(Last_Touch))

Note on attribution dataset: The attribution analysis uses a separate dataset (attribution_dataset.csv) containing 1,000 user journeys across 6 channels (Direct, Email, Organic, Paid Search, Paid Social, Referral), with user_purchase as the conversion indicator (10% conversion rate, n=100 converters). Four models are compared: Last-Touch, First-Touch, Linear, and Markov Chain (removal effect via absorbing Markov chain). The dataset contains journeys of 1–8 touchpoints (mean = 4.2).

Show code 
comparison_long <- comparison |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Last_Touch, First_Touch, Linear, Markov),
    names_to  = "Model",
    values_to = "Attribution"
  ) |>
  mutate(Model = factor(Model, levels = c("First_Touch", "Linear", "Last_Touch", "Markov")))

ggplot(comparison_long, aes(x = Channel, y = Attribution, fill = Model)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = position_dodge(width = 0.85),
           width = 0.8, na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(is.na(Attribution), "", paste0(round(Attribution, 1), "%"))),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.85),
    vjust = -0.4, size = 2.8, na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Channel", y = "Attribution (%)", fill = "Model",
    title = "Multi-Touch Attribution: Four Models Compared",
    caption = "Based on attribution_dataset.csv — 1,000 users, 100 converters"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "First_Touch" = "#59A14F",
    "Linear"      = "#F28E2B",
    "Last_Touch"  = "#4E79A7",
    "Markov"      = "#E15759"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
Show code 
comparison |>
  filter(!is.na(Markov)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Last_Touch, y = Markov, label = Channel)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "#E15759") +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  labs(
    x = "Last-Touch Attribution (%)",
    y = "Markov Attribution (%)",
    title = "Last-Touch vs. Markov: Which Channels Are Mis-Attributed?",
    caption = "Channels above the dashed line are undervalued by Last-Touch; below = overvalued"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

5.2.2 Model Comparison Table

Show code 
comparison |>
  mutate(
    LT_vs_Markov = round(Markov - Last_Touch, 2),
    Direction = case_when(
      is.na(LT_vs_Markov) ~ "—",
      LT_vs_Markov > 1    ~ paste0("▲ +", LT_vs_Markov, "%"),
      LT_vs_Markov < -1   ~ paste0("▼ ", LT_vs_Markov, "%"),
      TRUE                ~ "≈ Stable"
    )
  ) |>
  select(Channel, Last_Touch, First_Touch, Linear, Markov, Direction) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    Channel     = "Channel",
    Last_Touch  = "Last-Touch (%)",
    First_Touch = "First-Touch (%)",
    Linear      = "Linear (%)",
    Markov      = "Markov (%)",
    Direction   = "LT vs Markov"
  ) |>
  fmt_missing(columns = everything(), missing_text = "—") |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(Last_Touch, First_Touch, Linear, Markov), decimals = 1) |>
  data_color(columns = Markov, palette = "Reds") |>
  tab_header(title = "Attribution Model Comparison (All Four Models)") |>
  tab_options(table.width = pct(100))
Attribution Model Comparison (All Four Models)
Channel Last-Touch (%) First-Touch (%) Linear (%) Markov (%) LT vs Markov
Email 28.0 24.0 20.8 16.3 ▼ -11.69%
Direct 21.0 8.0 15.8 19.3 ▼ -1.71%
Organic 19.0 20.0 18.7 18.9 ≈ Stable
Paid Search 16.0 20.0 16.7 17.4 ▲ +1.42%
Referral 10.0 5.0 11.9 13.9 ▲ +3.92%
Paid Social 6.0 23.0 16.1 14.2 ▲ +8.21%

5.2.3 Key Insights from Attribution

Email is the most complex story. It ranks first in Last-Touch (28%) and First-Touch (24%), reflecting both high conversion closing power and strong journey initiation. However, the Markov model assigns it only 16.3% — the fourth highest — suggesting that Email’s Last-Touch dominance overstates its true incremental contribution. It is a high-value channel but not as uniquely critical as Last-Touch implies.

Direct receives 21% under Last-Touch but only 19.3% under Markov. It is relatively stable across models, suggesting it plays a genuine role in journeys — not purely as a last-step artifact, though some inflation remains.

Paid Social is the most dramatically undervalued channel under Last-Touch (6%) vs. Markov (14.2%). The First-Touch model assigns it 23%, its highest score, confirming it is a strong journey initiator. Paid Social consistently appears early in converting paths but receives almost no last-touch credit. This is the clearest case of systematic under-investment driven by Last-Touch reporting.

Organic and Paid Search are the most stable channels, varying by only ~2% across all four models. They contribute consistently at every stage of the funnel.

Referral is undervalued by Last-Touch (10%) and First-Touch (5%) relative to its Markov share (13.9%), indicating it plays a meaningful mid-funnel role that position-based models undercount.

6 Recommendations

The following recommendations are grounded in the attribution analysis and descriptive data. Each includes a confidence level and relevant caveat.

6.1 1. Increase Investment in Paid Social

Recommendation: Increase Paid Social budget allocation, treating it explicitly as an upper-funnel awareness channel.

Evidence: Paid Social receives only 6% under Last-Touch but 14.2% under Markov — a 2.4× increase. Its First-Touch share is 23%, the highest of any channel, confirming it is the primary journey initiator for converting users. It systematically loses credit to Email and Direct as those channels close the conversion.

Confidence: Moderate. The attribution dataset shows a consistent pattern, but Paid Social impressions from walled-garden platforms (Meta, LinkedIn) are not fully captured in click data, so the true contribution may be even higher.

Action: Increase Paid Social spend, add UTM parameters to all ad links, and track Paid Social as a top-of-funnel metric (reach, journey initiation) rather than a last-click conversion metric.

6.2 2. Maintain Email as a Conversion Channel

Recommendation: Keep Email campaigns strong for bottom-of-funnel re-engagement (application deadline reminders, event invites, follow-ups).

Evidence: Email leads Last-Touch attribution (28%) and First-Touch (24%), and ranks 4th in Markov (16.3%). It is effective at both initiating and closing journeys — the most versatile channel in the mix.

Confidence: High. Email’s strong performance is consistent across all four attribution models.

Action: Segment email lists by funnel stage. Use upper-funnel emails for prospects who arrived via Paid Social or Organic; use conversion-focused emails for users already engaged with the program.

6.3 3. Re-evaluate Direct as a Standalone Channel

Recommendation: Do not treat Direct traffic as an independent channel to invest in — it is primarily the closing step of journeys begun elsewhere.

Evidence: Direct receives 21% under Last-Touch but only 19.3% under Markov, and its First-Touch share is just 8% — the lowest of any channel. This confirms Direct rarely initiates journeys; it mainly captures users returning to convert after prior touchpoints.

Confidence: High. The First-Touch / Markov discrepancy is diagnostic of a closing-step channel.

Action: Focus investment on the channels (Paid Social, Organic, Email) that generate the journeys that Direct closes. Improve UTM tagging to reduce mis-attribution of untagged campaign traffic into Direct.

6.4 4. Invest in Referral as a Mid-Funnel Channel

Recommendation: Treat Referral as a mid-funnel nurturing channel and explore partnerships that generate referral traffic.

Evidence: Referral’s Markov share (13.9%) is notably higher than its Last-Touch share (10%) and its First-Touch share (5%), indicating it plays a meaningful role in the middle of converting journeys — keeping users in the funnel after initial discovery.

Confidence: Moderate. Referral is a heterogeneous category (partner sites, listings, directories) and its sources are not broken out in this dataset.

Action: Identify the top referral sources, prioritize partnerships with high-Markov-value referrers, and ensure all referral links are UTM-tagged for channel-level attribution.

6.5 5. Rebalance Upper vs. Lower Funnel Spend

Recommendation: Shift some budget from lower-funnel channels (Email, Direct-associated) toward upper-funnel channels (Paid Social, Organic content, Referral partnerships).

Evidence: The Markov model consistently re-weights credit toward channels that appear early in journeys (Paid Social: +8.2pp vs Last-Touch; Referral: +3.9pp) and away from channels that appear late (Email: −11.7pp; Direct: −1.7pp).

Confidence: Moderate. Requires cost data (not currently available) to calculate ROI-adjusted reallocation.

Action: Add cost data to the pipeline and re-run attribution with weighted ROI metrics before committing to specific budget shifts.

6.6 5. Strengthen UTM Tagging

Recommendation: Audit and enforce UTM tagging across all outbound links (email campaigns, social posts, paid ads).

Evidence: A large proportion of Direct traffic is likely mis-attributed traffic from untagged campaigns. Improving tagging would immediately improve the accuracy of both last-touch and Markov models.

Confidence: High. This is a data quality fix with no downside risk.

Action: Implement a UTM naming convention standard and verify all active campaign links before next semester’s recruitment cycle.

7 Limitations

7.1 Data Constraints

Constraint Impact
8 snapshot days only Trends are directional; no seasonality, no weekly patterns
user_id largely missing Cross-device stitching not possible; one user on two devices = two pseudo-users
No conversion events Attribution is modeled from page-view sequences as proxy; not actual conversions
Nested GA4 fields excluded event_params and ecommerce arrays were flattened out; no page-level or product-level detail
No session reconstruction Without timestamp-based sessionization, session-level metrics (bounce rate, session duration) are unavailable

7.2 Attribution-Specific Constraints

Constraint Impact
Walled-garden data gaps Paid Social impressions (Meta, LinkedIn) are not fully recorded in GA4 click data; Markov may undercount Paid Social
Small journey paths Markov chains require sufficient multi-touchpoint sequences; small datasets increase variance in removal effect estimates
No cost or ROI data Attribution percentages cannot be converted to budget recommendations without spend data
First-touch vs. last-touch vs. Markov No single model is “correct”; Markov is the most theoretically sound for multi-step journeys but not immune to data quality issues

7.3 What This Report Cannot Claim

This report cannot claim:

  • That Paid Social caused conversions — only that it appears in more conversion paths than last-touch suggests.
  • That the trends observed over 8 days are representative of the full academic year.
  • That the Markov attribution percentages are stable — with more data, they would likely shift.

8 Reproducibility

8.1 How to Reproduce This Report

  1. Clone the repository:

    git clone https://github.com/hsujeffrey/ibm6530-group4-project
cd ibm6530-group4-project

  2. Ensure data is available: Place events_snapshot.csv in data/. The Parquet file will be auto-built on first render.

  3. Install R packages:

    install.packages(c(
  "arrow", "dplyr", "ggplot2", "scales", "lubridate",
  "stringr", "tidyr", "forcats", "fs", "here",
  "readr", "gt", "knitr", "gridExtra", "ggrepel"
))

  4. Render the report:

    quarto render IBM6530_Final_Group4_MSDM_Odoo.qmd

8.2 File Structure

ibm6530-group4-project/
├── data/
│   ├── events_snapshot.csv               # GA4 flat export from BigQuery
│   ├── attribution_dataset.csv           # 1,000-user journey dataset for attribution
│   └── msdm_snapshot/events_flat/
│       └── events_snapshot.parquet       # Auto-generated on first render
├── IBM6530_Final_Group4_MSDM_Odoo.qmd    # This report
├── dashboard.qmd                         # Deliverable 1 — Dashboard
└── presentation.qmd                      # Deliverable 3 — Revealjs Presentation
